turns.content: Is the Mercator projection really that misleading? Show me how wrong our mental model of country sizes is.

Yes—Mercator is “honest” about one thing (local angles) and wildly “dishonest” about another (area). It preserves shape locally (conformal), which is why it’s useful for navigation and why coastlines “look right.” But it inflates area dramatically as you move away from the equator, because the projection’s scale factor grows like sec(latitude). That means at 60° latitude everything is stretched by 2× in linear dimensions and 4× in area; at 80° latitude the linear scale is ~5.76× and area is ~33×. A few mental-model shatters: - Greenland vs Africa: On many wall maps Greenland looks comparable to Africa. In reality Africa is about 30.37 million km² and Greenland is about 2.16 million km²—Africa is ~14× larger. - Europe vs South America: Europe looks huge on Mercator; South America looks modest. South America (~17.84 million km²) is almost twice Europe (~10.18 million km²). - Alaska vs Mexico: Alaska often looks comparable to Mexico. Mexico (~1.96 million km²) is actually larger than Alaska (~1.72 million km²). - Russia vs Africa: Russia (~17.1 million km²) looks monstrous (and it is large), but Africa is still almost twice Russia. If you want a visceral demonstration: imagine a “true area” map where every country’s area is proportional. That’s what equal-area projections (Gall–Peters, Mollweide, Eckert IV, etc.) aim for. Mercator instead makes the world’s high-latitude countries feel dominant and makes equatorial regions feel smaller than they are.
I know the “Greenland vs Africa” meme, but I want the mechanism. Like, what does “sec(latitude)” really mean in practice? Why does area blow up?

Mechanism in plain math: On the sphere, take latitude φ. On Earth, a small north–south distance corresponds to an angular change dφ; a small east–west distance corresponds to cos(φ) dλ (because circles of latitude shrink by cos φ). Mercator’s goal: preserve angles. To do that, the map must scale north–south and east–west by the same factor at a given latitude. East–west on the globe already shrinks with cos φ, but on the map you want equal scaling in both directions. So you compensate: you inflate east–west by 1/cos φ = sec φ. And to keep angles, you also inflate north–south by the same factor. That’s the key. So the linear scale factor k(φ) = sec φ. Area scale factor is the product of the two perpendicular linear scales: k² = sec² φ. Concrete numbers: - At φ = 0°: sec 0 = 1. No inflation. - At 30°: sec 30 ≈ 1.155. Area factor ≈ 1.333. Already 33% bigger. - At 45°: sec 45 ≈ 1.414. Area factor ≈ 2.0. Twice the area. - At 60°: sec 60 = 2. Area factor = 4. Four times the area. - At 75°: sec 75 ≈ 3.864. Area factor ≈ 14.93. ~15×. - At 80°: sec 80 ≈ 5.758. Area factor ≈ 33.15. ~33×. As φ → 90°, cos φ → 0, so sec φ → ∞. That’s why the poles can’t appear on a finite Mercator map; they’re at infinity in y. The “blow up” isn’t a bug; it’s the mathematical price of conformality on a cylinder tangent at the equator.
Can you show me the actual equations? Like, what are x and y for Mercator, and why does y go to infinity?

Sure. For a spherical Earth (radius R), with longitude λ (radians) and latitude φ (radians), the classic Mercator projection is: x = R (λ − λ₀) y = R ln( tan(π/4 + φ/2) ) Here λ₀ is the central meridian. Why y → ∞ at the poles: As φ → +π/2 (90°), π/4 + φ/2 → π/4 + π/4 = π/2, so tan(π/2) → ∞. ln(∞) → ∞. So y diverges. As φ → −π/2, π/4 + φ/2 → π/4 − π/4 = 0, tan(0) = 0, ln(0) → −∞. So y → −∞. This y is basically the “isometric latitude,” a function that increases without bound as you approach the poles, exactly matching the sec φ scaling needed for conformality. On an ellipsoid (more accurate Earth model), y uses a more complex formula involving eccentricity e, but the infinite behavior at ±90° remains.
I want the mind-blown part: if I take two countries at different latitudes with the same true area, how different do they look on Mercator?

If two regions have equal true area and are “small enough” that we can approximate them by their latitude (so distortion doesn’t vary much across them), then their apparent areas on Mercator scale roughly by sec² φ. So if region A is centered at latitude φA and region B at φB, then: (apparent area A) / (apparent area B) ≈ sec²(φA) / sec²(φB) Example: one country at 10° and another at 60°: - sec²(10°) ≈ (1 / cos 10°)² ≈ (1 / 0.9848)² ≈ 1.031 - sec²(60°) = 4 Ratio: 4 / 1.031 ≈ 3.88 So the 60° country looks almost 4× bigger than the 10° country even though they’re equal in reality. Even more dramatic: 0° vs 75°: - sec²(0°) = 1 - sec²(75°) ≈ 14.93 So the 75° region looks ~15× bigger than an equatorial region of the same true area. This is why equatorial Africa, Indonesia, and northern South America feel “smaller” than Scandinavia, Canada, and Russia on Mercator—your eyes are reading map area as physical area, but Mercator’s area is a completely different quantity.
Okay, but countries aren’t tiny patches. Distortion varies across a big country. How do we handle that? Like Russia spans huge latitudes.

Great point: for big regions you can’t treat distortion as constant. The correct way is to integrate the local area scale factor over the region. On Mercator (sphere), local area scale factor at latitude φ is sec² φ. If you had the country’s footprint in geographic coordinates, you’d compute: Apparent area on map ∝ ∬_region sec²(φ) dA_true Where dA_true is the true surface area element on the sphere: dA_true = R² cos φ dφ dλ. Then the map area element dA_map is: dA_map = (scale_x * scale_y) dA_true = sec² φ * dA_true And since dA_true already includes cos φ, you can see the cancellation structure: dA_map ∝ ∬ sec² φ * (cos φ dφ dλ) = ∬ sec φ dφ dλ (up to constants) So higher latitudes contribute disproportionately. For Russia: it spans roughly 41°N to 82°N. The northern parts are extremely inflated on Mercator; the southern parts less so. This makes the top of Russia look enormous. A practical workflow (what cartographers/GIS people do): 1) Represent the country as a polygon in lon/lat. 2) Project it to Mercator (x, y). 3) Compute polygon area in projected meters² (on the map plane). 4) Compare with geodesic area on ellipsoid. That gives the exact “looks bigger by factor X” for that projection, for that specific shape.
Give me a concrete “factor X” for a real country. Like, what’s the Mercator area inflation for, say, the UK vs Indonesia?

We can approximate with representative latitudes, but the exact factor depends on the country’s footprint. Still, a back-of-envelope is surprisingly revealing. Take rough “mid-latitudes”: - UK: centered ~54°N - Indonesia: centered ~2°S to 5°S; call it ~0–5° for roughness, say 2° Compute sec²: UK at 54°: cos 54° ≈ 0.5878 sec² ≈ (1/0.5878)² ≈ (1.701)² ≈ 2.894 Indonesia at 2°: cos 2° ≈ 0.99939 sec² ≈ 1.0012 So per unit true area, the UK’s patch looks ~2.9× bigger than Indonesia’s patch on Mercator. Now reality check with true areas: - UK ~243,610 km² - Indonesia ~1,904,569 km² (about 7.8× larger) If you naively “read” Mercator map areas, you’d unconsciously discount Indonesia and amplify the UK. Multiply true areas by the rough inflation factors: - UK apparent ∝ 243,610 * 2.9 ≈ 706,000 “units” - Indonesia apparent ∝ 1,904,569 * 1.0 ≈ 1,907,000 “units” Indonesia still looks bigger, but only ~2.7× bigger instead of ~7.8×. That’s the mental-model distortion: relative size differences compress or invert depending on latitude.
That’s wild. Now show me the opposite: a projection that preserves area. How does it “pay” for that? What gets distorted instead?

Equal-area projections keep area correct locally (area scale factor = 1 everywhere), but they must sacrifice either angles (shape), distances, or directions. The “payment” usually shows up as: - Shapes: continents look “squashed” or “stretched.” Angles are not preserved, so coastlines can look less familiar. - Directions: bearings are not constant like on Mercator. - Distances: no flat map can preserve all distances; equal-area ones typically have varying distance distortion. Example: Mollweide (equal-area, pseudo-cylindrical): - Area is correct. - Meridians are curved; shapes near the edges get stretched horizontally. - The world looks more “oval,” and familiar high-latitude regions look less dominant. Gall–Peters (a specific cylindrical equal-area): - Straight meridians and parallels like Mercator (visually similar grid). - But shapes are heavily distorted: near the equator things look vertically stretched; near higher latitudes things look horizontally stretched. - It’s a very blunt demonstration of how “shape familiarity” is not the same as truth. Equal-area projections answer “How much?” truthfully. Mercator answers “Which way?” and “What angle?” truthfully.
I always hear “no map is perfect,” but that sounds like a cop-out. Can we formalize what’s impossible?

Yes: Gauss’s Theorema Egregium implies you can’t flatten a curved surface onto a plane without distortion of intrinsic geometry. The sphere has constant positive Gaussian curvature; the plane has zero curvature. You cannot have an isometry between them (a distance-preserving mapping). From that, you get impossibility results: - You can’t preserve area and angles everywhere simultaneously (except on developable surfaces like a cylinder, but a sphere isn’t developable). - You can’t preserve all distances. - You can’t preserve shapes globally. So map design is optimization: decide which properties matter for the intended use and minimize the rest. Cartographers talk in terms of Tissot’s indicatrix: imagine an infinitesimal circle on the globe. A projection maps it to an ellipse. The ellipse encodes distortion: - If it stays a circle: conformal (angles preserved). - If ellipse area is constant: equal-area. - If ellipses are small overall: low distortion. Different projections produce different fields of ellipses.
Tissot’s indicatrix is exactly the kind of thing I want. Describe what it looks like on Mercator vs Mollweide vs Robinson.

Think of sprinkling tiny circles across the Earth and projecting them. Mercator: - Every indicatrix is a circle (conformal), but the circles grow rapidly with latitude. - Near the equator: small circles. - By 60°: circles are 2× radius (4× area) compared to equator. - Near 80°: huge circles. So you get “perfect shape locally,” but wildly varying scale. Mollweide (equal-area): - Every indicatrix has the same area, but they become increasingly elliptical away from the center. - Near the central meridian and mid-latitudes: moderate ellipses. - Toward the edges (near ±180°): ellipses stretch horizontally, compress vertically. So “honest area,” “shape drifts.” Robinson (compromise): - Indicatrices are neither circles nor equal-area. - Distortion is “smoothed” to look pleasing: moderate size variation, moderate ellipticity. - No single truth; it’s designed to avoid extreme ugliness. Robinson is popular for general-purpose world maps because it looks balanced, but it’s not preserving a specific metric property.
I want to really feel the sec² distortion. Can we do a thought experiment with a 1,000 km by 1,000 km square on the ground at different latitudes? What does it look like on Mercator?

Approximate the square as small enough that scale is constant across it. On the globe, it’s a 1000 km × 1000 km true square (area 1,000,000 km²). On Mercator at latitude φ, both north–south and east–west linear scales are multiplied by sec φ, so the map depicts it as: (1000 * sec φ) km by (1000 * sec φ) km in map units (scaled). So apparent area becomes: 1,000,000 * sec² φ km² (in relative map-area units). Examples: - At 0°: still 1000×1000, area 1,000,000. - At 45°: sec 45 ≈ 1.414 → looks ~1414×1414 → area ~2,000,000. - At 60°: sec 60 = 2 → looks 2000×2000 → area 4,000,000. - At 75°: sec 75 ≈ 3.864 → looks 3864×3864 → area ~14,930,000. So a “million km²” patch at 75° latitude visually occupies the same map area as almost fifteen “million km²” patches at the equator.
That makes me wonder: is there a projection that keeps scale constant along certain lines, like a compromise between Mercator and equal-area?

Many projections are designed to have true scale along specific lines (standard lines). For cylindrical projections: - Mercator: true scale at the equator only. - Transverse Mercator: true scale along a chosen central meridian (used for UTM zones). - Secant versions: the cylinder intersects the globe, giving two standard lines where scale is true and distortion is minimized between them. For conic projections: - Lambert Conformal Conic: true scale along one or two standard parallels; conformal. - Albers Equal-Area Conic: equal-area with one or two standard parallels; often used for mid-latitude countries/continents. For azimuthal projections: - Azimuthal Equidistant: distances true from the center point. - Stereographic: conformal, often used for polar regions. So yes: you can “place” the low-distortion region where you care about it, but you can’t make distortion disappear everywhere.
Okay, now I want to talk about “mental model.” Why do people internalize Mercator so strongly? Is it just because it’s in web maps?

Web maps amplified it, but the mental model is older and deeper: 1) Historical dominance in education: For decades, classroom wall maps and atlases used Mercator or Mercator-like projections, especially in places where maritime navigation history mattered. 2) Visual familiarity: Conformal projections preserve local shapes, so coastlines look “right” to the eye. Humans are sensitive to shape cues and less sensitive to area cues. 3) Rectangular convenience: A rectangular world map is easy to print, frame, and index with a simple grid. Mercator is a rectangle with straight meridians and parallels. 4) Web tiling: The dominant “Web Mercator” (EPSG:3857) is computationally convenient for square tiles and fast rendering. That cemented Mercator as the default interactive world view. 5) Cognitive bias: People equate “bigger on the page” with “bigger in reality.” If the most common depiction inflates higher latitudes, you internalize an inflated sense of those regions. So it’s not that Mercator is “evil”; it’s that its strengths align with publishing and navigation, and its weaknesses align with human misinterpretation.
Wait—Web Mercator. Is it the same as classic Mercator? I’ve heard it’s “wrong” even compared to true Mercator.

Web Mercator is a close cousin but not identical. Classic Mercator can be defined on a sphere or on an ellipsoid. The ellipsoidal Mercator uses the ellipsoid’s eccentricity to compute y (so north–south scaling matches conformality on the ellipsoid). Web Mercator (EPSG:3857) simplifies by treating Earth as a sphere with radius 6378137 m (the WGS84 semi-major axis) and uses the spherical Mercator formulas: x = R λ y = R ln( tan(π/4 + φ/2) ) This means: - It’s not truly conformal on the ellipsoid (but close). - It introduces small additional scale errors relative to ellipsoidal Mercator, especially at high latitudes. - It clamps latitude to about ±85.05113° so y stays finite for tiling. So “wrong” depends on what you mean: it’s a pragmatic standard optimized for web mapping performance and consistency, not for geodetic purity.
That clamp number—85.05113°—feels oddly specific. Where does it come from?

It’s chosen so that the projected map fits neatly into a square in the common tile scheme. In Web Mercator, if you map longitude ±180° to x = ±πR, you get a width of 2πR. For a square map, you want the y extent to match that: y should range from −πR to +πR. Set y = πR and solve for φ: πR = R ln( tan(π/4 + φ/2) ) π = ln( tan(π/4 + φ/2) ) e^π = tan(π/4 + φ/2) π/4 + φ/2 = arctan(e^π) φ = 2 arctan(e^π) − π/2 Compute that and you get φ ≈ 1.484422229 rad ≈ 85.05112878°. So it’s the latitude at which the Mercator y equals πR, making the world map a perfect square in projected coordinates.
I’m trying to build intuition for other projections too. Can you give me a “projection personality profile” for a bunch of common ones?

Sure—here’s a compact “personality profile” list: 1) Mercator (conformal cylindrical) - Truth: local angles/shapes, compass bearings as straight lines (loxodromes). - Lies: area, especially high latitudes; poles infinite. - Vibe: “navigation grid, web map default.” 2) Transverse Mercator (conformal, cylinder rotated) - Truth: angles; good for north–south regions. - Lies: scale grows away from central meridian. - Vibe: “surveying, UTM zones.” 3) Lambert Conformal Conic (conformal conic) - Truth: angles; great for mid-latitude east–west regions. - Lies: area; distortion increases away from standard parallels. - Vibe: “aeronautical charts, national maps.” 4) Albers Equal-Area Conic (equal-area) - Truth: area; good for thematic mapping (population density, climate). - Lies: shapes/angles. - Vibe: “data honesty for continents.” 5) Mollweide (equal-area) - Truth: global area. - Lies: shapes near edges. - Vibe: “oval world, serious thematic maps.” 6) Eckert IV (equal-area) - Truth: area; visually pleasing. - Lies: shapes; some stretching. - Vibe: “balanced equal-area.” 7) Winkel Tripel (compromise) - Truth: minimizes combined distortion (area, direction, distance) heuristically. - Lies: everything a bit, nothing catastrophically. - Vibe: “atlas standard.” 8) Robinson (compromise) - Truth: aesthetics, reduced extremes. - Lies: not metric-faithful. - Vibe: “friendly world map.” 9) Azimuthal Equidistant (distance-from-center) - Truth: distances and directions from the center point. - Lies: everything far from center; edges distort. - Vibe: “radio range maps, polar views.” 10) Stereographic (conformal azimuthal) - Truth: angles; circles map to circles. - Lies: area. - Vibe: “polar navigation, star charts.” 11) Gnomonic (great circles as straight lines) - Truth: great-circle routes are straight. - Lies: everything else; extreme distortion away from center. - Vibe: “route planning, geometric magic trick.”
Gnomonic sounds like a magic trick. Explain why great circles become straight lines. I want the geometry.

Gnomonic is literally central projection from the center of the sphere onto a tangent plane. Picture: - A sphere. - A plane tangent to the sphere at some point (the projection center on the surface). - For any point on the sphere, draw a line from the sphere’s center through that point; where it hits the tangent plane is the projected point. Now the key fact: - Great circles are intersections of the sphere with planes that pass through the sphere’s center. - In gnomonic projection, those planes (through the center) intersect the tangent plane in a straight line (because intersection of two planes is a line). - Points on the great circle lie in that plane, so their projection rays lie in that same plane, so their projected points lie along the line of intersection. Hence: every great circle maps to a straight line segment (where it’s within the hemisphere visible from the tangent point). That’s why gnomonic charts were used to plot shortest paths: draw a straight line, then transfer waypoints to a different projection for navigation.
Mind-blown. Now do the opposite: is there a projection where loxodromes are straight lines besides Mercator?

Mercator is the canonical one where all rhumb lines (loxodromes—paths of constant compass bearing) are straight. There are other conformal projections where some families of loxodromes appear straight under certain conditions, but “all loxodromes are straight” is essentially a defining feature of Mercator among common projections. Reason: On the sphere, loxodromes spiral toward the poles, crossing meridians at a constant angle. Mercator’s conformality plus its specific y(φ) function makes that constant-angle property become constant slope in x–y space. In other projections, even if they are conformal, the relationship between latitude and y is different, so constant-bearing curves don’t become straight universally. So if your requirement is “any constant-bearing line is straight,” Mercator is basically the answer.
I’m also curious about how projections handle Antarctica. On Mercator it’s like a monster. What projections make it look “reasonable,” and what do they sacrifice?

Antarctica is a perfect stress test because it sits at extreme latitude and wraps around the pole. Ways to make it “reasonable”: 1) Polar azimuthal projections (best for polar regions) - Stereographic (conformal): Antarctica looks shape-faithful locally; size inflates away from the pole (outer edge). - Lambert Azimuthal Equal-Area: Antarctica’s area is correct; shape distorts but is often acceptable. - Azimuthal Equidistant: distances from the pole are correct; good for range rings. 2) Interrupted projections (reduce ocean stretching) - Goode’s Interrupted Homolosine (equal-area): often used for thematic maps; continents are less stretched because the map is “cut” over oceans. Antarctica gets split or placed along the bottom in a less dominant way. 3) Compromise world maps - Winkel Tripel, Robinson: Antarctica is still distorted, but less grotesquely than Mercator because the projection compresses high latitudes vertically and doesn’t send the pole to infinity. The sacrifice depends: - If you choose equal-area: you sacrifice angles/shape. - If you choose conformal: you sacrifice area. - If you choose interrupted: you sacrifice continuity (the world is cut).
Tell me more about interrupted projections. How do you decide where to “cut” the world?

Interrupted projections are like admitting: “I can’t keep the whole world continuous without ugly distortion, so I’ll cut where it hurts least.” Design principles: 1) Cut mostly through oceans: You preserve the shapes of continents—useful for thematic maps where land patterns matter. 2) Avoid cutting through important regions: If the audience needs to see certain connections (say, across Eurasia), keep those intact. 3) Reduce extreme stretching near the edges: Many pseudo-cylindrical projections stretch features near ±180° longitude. Interruptions break the map into lobes so no lobe extends too far from its central meridian. Example: Goode’s Interrupted Homolosine: - It blends Sinusoidal (good near equator) and Mollweide (good for outer latitudes) while keeping equal area. - The interruptions are arranged to keep major landmasses in their own lobes. The cost: - You can’t easily trace continuous paths across cuts. - Oceans look fragmented. - It’s not great for navigation or anything requiring continuity. But for “how is rainfall distributed globally?” or “where is population density highest?” it’s excellent.
I want to go super technical for a bit. How do you measure “overall distortion” of a projection? Like, is there a metric?

There isn’t one universally “correct” metric because distortion has multiple dimensions (area, angle, distance, direction). But cartographers use several quantitative approaches: 1) Areal distortion metric - Local area scale factor A(φ,λ) = determinant of the Jacobian of the projection (normalized). - You can compute statistics over the globe: mean, RMS, max, or log-based measures (since scale factors multiply). 2) Angular distortion - For conformality, measure deviation from orthogonality or compare principal scale factors. - In Tissot terms: if the indicatrix ellipse’s axes are equal, angular distortion is zero. 3) Distance distortion - Sample many pairs of points, compare map distance vs geodesic distance (after choosing a map scale normalization). - Compute RMS relative error. 4) Combined “compromise” metrics - Some design projections explicitly minimize an objective function combining area, angle, and distance errors (often weighted). - Winkel Tripel, for example, was designed to minimize a combination of three distortions (hence “Tripel”) in a heuristic way. 5) Flexion and skewness (more advanced) - Measures of how distortion changes locally (important for visual smoothness). In practice, you pick a region of interest and a use case, then choose a metric aligned with what matters: equal-area for choropleths, conformal for shape and bearings, equidistant for range, etc.
Jacobian determinant—nice. Can you show the Jacobian for Mercator and how it leads to sec²?

For spherical Mercator: x(λ,φ) = R λ y(λ,φ) = R ln( tan(π/4 + φ/2) ) Compute partial derivatives: ∂x/∂λ = R ∂x/∂φ = 0 For y, derivative with respect to φ: Let f(φ) = ln( tan(π/4 + φ/2) ) df/dφ = (1 / tan(...)) * sec²(...) * (1/2) But there’s a known simplification: df/dφ = sec φ So: ∂y/∂φ = R sec φ ∂y/∂λ = 0 So the Jacobian matrix (λ,φ → x,y) is: J = [[R, 0], [0, R sec φ]] Determinant det(J) = R² sec φ. But careful: (λ,φ) are angular coordinates, not equal-length coordinates on the sphere. The true area element on the sphere is R² cos φ dλ dφ. Map area element is det(J) dλ dφ = R² sec φ dλ dφ. So the ratio: dA_map / dA_true = (R² sec φ dλ dφ) / (R² cos φ dλ dφ) = sec φ / cos φ = sec² φ. That’s the clean derivation: the Jacobian gives you how the map scales angular coordinates; dividing by the sphere’s own metric factor cos φ yields the true areal distortion.
Okay, now I want to step back and talk about how projections shape geopolitics in people’s heads. Without getting into politics-politics, just the cognitive effect.

Cognitively, projection choice can shift perceived prominence in three big ways: 1) Relative area salience - If high-latitude regions are inflated, they feel “more substantial,” “more dominant,” or “more important” simply because they occupy more visual real estate. - Conversely, equatorial regions can feel minimized. 2) Centrality and marginality - What’s centered on the map feels central to the world. - A map centered on the Greenwich meridian frames the world differently than one centered on the Pacific. - Edge effects: regions near the map boundary feel peripheral or “cut off.” 3) Shape familiarity - People often treat “looks like the familiar outline” as a proxy for correctness. - Conformal projections can reinforce that feeling by keeping local shapes recognizable, even when area is wildly off. Even without any explicit messaging, these visual cues can subtly bias intuition: “That region is huge,” “that region is far away,” “the world is oriented this way,” etc. It’s a reminder that maps are not neutral pictures—they’re mathematical transformations with perceptual consequences.
Speaking of central meridians: what happens if we rotate Mercator so that the Pacific is centered? Does that change distortion in any meaningful way?

Rotating the central meridian in Mercator changes what’s in the middle vs at the edges, but it doesn’t change the latitude-dependent distortion pattern. Mercator’s scale factor depends only on latitude, not longitude. So: - Area inflation at 60°N is still 4× no matter where you center. - What changes is which landmasses get split at the ±180° boundary and which are displayed contiguously. Centering on the Pacific: - Keeps Asia and the Americas on opposite sides, often showing the Pacific as a continuous ocean. - Europe/Africa may get split near the edges depending on exact centering. - It can change the “story” of connectivity (Atlantic vs Pacific emphasis) without changing the mathematical distortion magnitude. So it’s a perceptual re-framing rather than a distortion fix.
I want to talk about “projection as storytelling.” How do cartographers decide for an atlas vs a thematic map vs a navigation chart?

It’s a design problem with constraints: 1) Navigation charts - Need accurate angles/bearings locally; conformal projections dominate. - Mercator for marine navigation historically; Transverse Mercator and Lambert Conformal Conic for aviation and surveying depending on orientation. 2) Atlases / general reference world maps - Need “balanced” appearance and readability. - Compromise projections like Winkel Tripel (common in modern atlases) reduce extreme distortions and look familiar. 3) Thematic maps (choropleths, density maps) - Need area honesty so visual comparisons are meaningful. - Equal-area projections are preferred: Albers for mid-latitudes, Mollweide/Eckert/Equal Earth for global themes. 4) Regional maps - Choose projection tuned to region’s shape and latitude range. - Example: Albers for a wide east–west country; Transverse Mercator for a tall north–south region; local azimuthal for a city/region around a point. 5) Special-purpose maps - Great-circle route maps: gnomonic (for drawing), then transfer. - Distance-from-point: azimuthal equidistant. - Satellite imagery: often uses specific projections tied to sensor geometry. The “storytelling” is: what question is the map answering? Then pick the projection that makes that question visually truthful.
You mentioned Equal Earth. I’ve seen it online and it looks… oddly pleasing. What’s special about it compared to older equal-area projections?

Equal Earth is a modern equal-area projection designed specifically to look familiar to people used to compromise projections like Robinson, while still preserving area. What’s special: - It’s equal-area, so choropleths and area comparisons are legitimate. - Its outline resembles the “rounded rectangle” aesthetic of popular world maps. - It reduces the “stretched” look that some older equal-area projections have, especially in the mid-latitudes. Technically: - It’s a pseudo-cylindrical projection defined by polynomial equations relating latitude to y, tuned so that the resulting graticule (grid) feels balanced. - It keeps the equator straight, central meridian straight, and other meridians symmetrically curved. So it’s an example of projection design that explicitly acknowledges human perception: “Let’s be truthful about area without making the world look alien.”
I want to do a little projection safari. Can we pick one country—say, Brazil—and describe how it morphs across several projections?

Brazil is a great test because it’s large, mostly tropical, and spans a moderate latitude range in the Southern Hemisphere. Mercator: - Brazil looks somewhat smaller than you’d expect if you’re used to Europe/Canada looking huge. - Northern Brazil near the equator is close to true scale; southern Brazil is slightly inflated, but not dramatically. - Shapes look familiar (conformal), coastlines “feel right.” Mollweide (equal-area): - Brazil’s area looks more “correct” relative to higher-latitude countries. - Shape becomes slightly flattened or broadened depending on where it sits relative to the projection’s center; not too bad because it’s not near the extreme edges. Winkel Tripel (compromise): - Brazil looks quite natural. - It’s one reason this projection is popular: mid-latitude/tropical landmasses look reasonable. Gall–Peters (cylindrical equal-area): - Brazil appears taller/narrower than you might expect, especially near the equator where vertical stretching is noticeable. - Area is correct, but the shape can feel “pulled.” Lambert Azimuthal Equal-Area (centered on Brazil): - Brazil looks extremely faithful in area and fairly good in shape near the center. - Distortion grows outward; other continents warp more. So Brazil is relatively stable across many projections compared to something like Greenland or Antarctica, which swing wildly depending on projection.
Now do Greenland. I want the dramatic shape-shift.

Greenland is projection drama personified because it sits high in latitude and has a distinctive outline. Mercator: - Looks gigantic; area is massively inflated. - Shape looks “plausible” (conformal), which is part of the trap: it looks like a big familiar island, not a distorted blob. Mollweide: - Area shrinks to a more realistic proportion relative to Africa. - Shape becomes more squashed; it can look wider and less “upright.” Equal Earth: - Similar: area looks right; shape changes but remains recognizable. - Greenland no longer dominates the North Atlantic visually. Stereographic polar (centered on North Pole): - Greenland’s shape can look quite good if it’s near the center of the projection. - But its area can still be exaggerated depending on distance from the center. Lambert Azimuthal Equal-Area (polar): - Greenland’s area is correct; shape is decent near the pole. The key psychological twist: Mercator makes Greenland both big and “shape-familiar,” which feels authoritative. Equal-area projections make it smaller but can make it look less familiar, so people sometimes misinterpret equal-area as “distorting Greenland,” when in fact it’s correcting the area.
That’s such a good point: people treat familiarity as truth. How would you convince someone, visually, that equal-area isn’t “wrong”?

A strong visual argument uses invariants and comparisons: 1) Overlay equal-area reference shapes - Put equal-area circles (same true area) at different latitudes and show they remain equal in area on an equal-area map, while they explode on Mercator. 2) Use a cartogram-like check - Show a set of countries with known area ratios and test whether the map visually matches those ratios. - Example: Africa vs Greenland, South America vs Europe, etc. 3) Show Tissot’s indicatrix - On Mercator: circles grow with latitude. - On equal-area: ellipses change shape but keep constant area. It reframes “distortion” as a trade: you can have circle-shape or circle-area, not both. 4) Side-by-side with numbers - Put the actual areas next to the shapes on each projection. - People accept the correction when the numeric truth is anchored. 5) Explain what “wrong” means - “Wrong for area comparisons” vs “wrong for angle/bearing tasks.” Equal-area isn’t “less correct”; it’s correct about a different property.
Let’s get into the weeds of graticules. Why do some projections have curved meridians and others straight? What determines that?

It comes down to the functional form of the projection equations and what constraints you impose. If you require: - Parallels (lines of constant latitude) to be straight horizontal lines, you’re in the cylindrical / pseudo-cylindrical family. - Meridians (constant longitude) to be straight vertical lines too, you get true cylindrical projections (Mercator, equirectangular, cylindrical equal-area). If you allow meridians to curve but keep parallels straight, you get pseudo-cylindrical projections (Mollweide, Sinusoidal, Eckert). Curved meridians can distribute distortion more evenly and avoid extreme stretching near the edges. If you make meridians converge (like a cone), you get conic projections: meridians are straight lines radiating from a point; parallels are arcs of circles. If you project onto a plane (azimuthal), meridians are typically straight lines from the center, and parallels are circles (or vice versa depending on orientation). So the graticule is a fingerprint of the surface you’re projecting onto (cylinder, cone, plane) plus any additional mathematical tweaks.
Equirectangular always looks “simple.” Is it ever a good idea?

Equirectangular (also called Plate Carrée when standard parallel is the equator) is: x = R λ y = R φ It’s simple and useful in some contexts: - Raster data storage: many global datasets are distributed in a simple lon/lat grid; Plate Carrée is straightforward for indexing. - Quick-and-dirty visualization where accuracy isn’t the goal. - Some analyses where you’re working in angular units and know what you’re doing. But as a map for human interpretation, it’s often worse than Mercator in some ways: - It’s not conformal, so shapes distort. - It’s not equal-area, so areas distort (though differently than Mercator). - It massively stretches high latitudes horizontally because it doesn’t account for cos φ shrinkage of parallels. It’s “good” when computational simplicity and a rectangular grid are more important than geometric fidelity.
Can you compare equirectangular’s area distortion to Mercator’s?

On the sphere: Plate Carrée: x = R λ y = R φ Jacobian determinant det(J) = R² (since ∂x/∂λ=R, ∂y/∂φ=R). True area element on sphere: R² cos φ dλ dφ. So area scale factor: dA_map / dA_true = (R² dλ dφ) / (R² cos φ dλ dφ) = sec φ. So Plate Carrée inflates area by sec φ (not sec² φ). That’s still unbounded at the poles, but less aggressive than Mercator. Mercator inflates area by sec² φ, stronger. However, Plate Carrée also distorts shapes badly because x-scale doesn’t match y-scale: east–west scale factor is sec φ, north–south is 1. That anisotropy turns circles into ellipses with growing eccentricity. Mercator keeps circles circles (conformal) but grows them more in size.
So in Tissot terms: Plate Carrée has ellipses that get wider but not taller?

Exactly. At latitude φ: - East–west scale is proportional to 1/cos φ = sec φ (because one degree of longitude corresponds to cos φ on the globe but constant on the map). - North–south scale is constant (since y is proportional to φ directly). So indicatrices: - Same height everywhere (in terms of local scale). - Increasing width as you move poleward. They become more and more horizontally stretched ellipses, and their area increases like sec φ. Mercator: - Both scales increase equally as sec φ, so indicatrices remain circles but grow in size.
If Mercator is conformal, does that mean it preserves angles between any curves? Like, if two roads intersect at 30° on Earth, they intersect at 30° on the map?

Locally, yes—at the point of intersection. Conformal means it preserves angles between intersecting curves at a point (infinitesimally). So if two paths cross at some angle on the surface, the angle between their tangents at that point is preserved on the map. Caveats: - It’s local. Over long distances, the curves themselves can be very different, so “angle between endpoints” isn’t a thing; it’s about the intersection angle at a point. - On an ellipsoid vs sphere, you need the correct Mercator variant for true conformality on that surface. Web Mercator is very close but not exact. This is why conformal projections are loved for navigation and surveying: small shapes are correct up to scale, and bearings behave nicely.
I want to understand “bearing behaves nicely.” Why do rhumb lines become straight lines on Mercator? Give me the calculus intuition.

On the sphere, a rhumb line is a curve that crosses all meridians at a constant angle α (constant bearing relative to north). In Mercator coordinates: - x is proportional to longitude λ. - y is proportional to isometric latitude ψ, where dψ = sec φ dφ. The key relationship for a rhumb line is: dλ/dφ = tan α / cos φ  (up to sign conventions) Because to maintain constant angle with meridians, the east–west component must scale with cos φ. Now convert to Mercator: dx = R dλ dy = R dψ = R sec φ dφ So slope on the map is: dy/dx = (R sec φ dφ) / (R dλ) = (sec φ) / (dλ/dφ) Substitute dλ/dφ: dy/dx = (sec φ) / (tan α / cos φ) = (1/cos φ) / (tan α / cos φ) = 1 / tan α = cot α Constant. So the rhumb line has constant slope in (x,y), hence it’s a straight line. That’s the calculus “click”: Mercator’s y is engineered so that the cos φ factor cancels out.
Okay, now I’m thinking about map projection as “choosing coordinates.” Is that a fair framing?

Very fair. A projection is essentially a coordinate transformation from a curved surface coordinate system (latitude/longitude on a sphere/ellipsoid) to planar coordinates (x,y). Different projections choose different functions: x = f(λ, φ) y = g(λ, φ) And those functions encode what you want to preserve. In differential geometry terms: - The globe has a metric tensor describing distances. - The map plane has a Euclidean metric. - A projection pulls back/pushes forward that metric, producing distortion. Conformal projections preserve the metric up to a scalar factor (angles preserved). Equal-area preserve the area form. So “choosing coordinates” is exactly the right mental model—except that because the target is flat, the coordinate choice necessarily warps the metric.
I’d love a projection that makes the world look like a globe, but flat. Like, can you do that?

The closest you can get is an orthographic projection: it looks like a globe photographed from far away. Orthographic: - Projects points onto a plane as if viewing the Earth from infinite distance. - Shows one hemisphere at a time. - Preserves neither area nor angles globally, but it preserves the “visual impression” of a sphere. Other “globe-like” ones: - Perspective (generalized) projections: simulate a camera at finite distance. Used for realistic views. - Azimuthal projections centered on a point can also feel globe-like, especially stereographic or azimuthal equidistant. But you can’t show the whole world in a single globe-like view without either: - Hiding half (orthographic), or - Doing a compromise that departs from true perspective.
Azimuthal equidistant is used for that famous emblem map, right? Why is it special?

Azimuthal equidistant is special because: - Distances from the center point to any other point are true (on the sphere). - Directions (azimuths) from the center to any other point are also true. So it’s great for showing “how far from here to everywhere” or “what direction from here.” The graticule: - Meridians are straight lines radiating from the center. - Parallels are concentric circles. Distortion: - It grows as you move away from the center. - The outer edge (antipode) is extremely distorted, and you can’t represent the entire sphere without issues (the antipode maps to a circle boundary in some forms). It’s a projection with a very clear promise: “If you care about distances/directions from this point, trust this map.”
Can we talk about what happens at the antipode? That seems like a recurring “singularity” theme.

Yes—many projections have singularities or “problem points” where distortion becomes extreme or the mapping becomes undefined. In azimuthal projections: - You project onto a plane tangent at the center point. - Points on the far side of the globe are at steep angles relative to the tangent plane. - The antipode is maximally far; many formulas push it to infinity or to a boundary circle. Examples: - Stereographic: the antipode goes to infinity (because it’s projection from the opposite pole onto a plane). - Gnomonic: only one hemisphere is representable; beyond that, rays intersect the tangent plane “behind” the plane. - Azimuthal equidistant: the antipode is at radius πR from the center (finite), but distortion near it is huge; small neighborhoods around the antipode are stretched. These singularities are the geometric price of flattening: you often choose where to “put the pain”—poles, antipode, edges, or cuts.
I want to switch to a practical angle: if I’m making a thematic map of global population density, what projection should I pick, and why?

For population density, area fidelity is crucial—otherwise high-latitude regions look bigger and can visually dilute density, or equatorial regions look smaller and densities look exaggerated. So you want an equal-area projection. Good global choices: - Equal Earth: equal-area and visually familiar; excellent for modern thematic maps. - Mollweide: classic equal-area; good if you like the oval aesthetic. - Eckert IV: equal-area with a nice balance. - Interrupted Goode Homolosine: equal-area and reduces land distortion, but breaks oceans. If the map is specifically about continents rather than oceans, an interrupted equal-area can make landmasses look less stretched. If continuity matters (e.g., global flows), choose a non-interrupted equal-area like Equal Earth. Avoid Mercator for density choropleths; it systematically biases the visual interpretation.
What about mapping something like airline routes or global shipping lanes? Then what?

For routes, it depends on what property you want to emphasize: - If you want shortest paths (great circles): use gnomonic to draw straight great-circle segments, then transfer. - If you want a visually pleasing global route map: Winkel Tripel or Robinson can work as compromises. - If you want consistent bearings (rhumb lines): Mercator makes those straight, but global airline routes are usually great circles, not rhumb lines. - If you want distance-from-hub: azimuthal equidistant centered on the hub is perfect. So for “global connections” maps, compromise projections are common because they keep the world recognizable while not exploding the poles. For technical route planning, specialized projections are used.
I want to understand why great circles look curved on Mercator. Is there a simple explanation?

Yes: on Mercator, only rhumb lines are straight by design. Great circles generally change bearing as they travel (except the equator and meridians). Since Mercator turns constant bearing into constant slope, any path whose bearing changes will curve. Intuition: - On the globe, the shortest route bends toward the pole relative to a constant-bearing route. - Mercator stretches high latitudes enormously, so that bending shows up as a pronounced curve. A classic example: a route across the North Atlantic. The great circle arcs northward; on Mercator it appears as a curve bulging toward higher latitude. That’s not the route being “wrong”—it’s the projection’s geometry.
Okay, I want a list of “straight lines correspond to X” for different projections. Like a cheat sheet.

Cheat sheet of “straight line meaning”: - Mercator: straight line = rhumb line (constant bearing). - Gnomonic: straight line = great circle (shortest path on sphere). - Azimuthal equidistant (centered): straight radial line = constant azimuth from center; distance along it is true from center. - Stereographic (centered): circles on sphere map to circles/lines; not a simple “straight line = X” for routes, but it preserves angles. - Lambert Conformal Conic: straight line is not generally a special geodesic, but local angles are preserved; small segments behave well. - Equirectangular: straight lines correspond to linear relationships in lat/lon, not a natural navigation path; it’s more of a coordinate grid. In general, “straight line” on a map corresponds to geodesics on the developable surface used in the projection (cylinder/cone/plane), not necessarily geodesics on the sphere.
That last sentence is interesting. Can you unpack it?

Think of a projection as: (sometimes) projecting the globe onto a developable surface (cylinder, cone, plane), then unrolling that surface to a plane. On a cylinder: - Geodesics on the cylinder unroll to straight lines on the plane. - Mercator’s magic is that rhumb lines become straight, which is a different property than cylinder geodesics; it’s tied to conformality and the specific y scaling. But the general idea holds: some families of curves become straight because of how the surface unrolls. On a cone: - Great circles don’t map to straight lines generally, but certain loxodromic or constant azimuth lines relative to the cone’s apex can have simpler forms. On a plane (azimuthal): - Great circles through the center can sometimes map to straight lines depending on the projection (gnomonic does this for all great circles, but only within a hemisphere). So “straightness” is not inherently meaningful unless you know the projection’s geometry and which curves are mapped to straightness by its construction.
I want to do a distortion “heat map” conceptually. Where is Mercator least distorted? Where is it most distorted?

Mercator distortion depends only on latitude: Least distorted: - Near the equator (φ ≈ 0°). Scale factor ≈ 1. Increasing distortion: - As you move toward ±90°. It grows smoothly but accelerates. Most distorted: - Approaching the poles. In theory infinite at ±90°. - In Web Mercator, the cutoff at ±85.051° is still enormously distorted: scale factor sec(85.051°) ≈ about 11.5, so area factor ≈ 132. So a conceptual heat map would be horizontal bands: - Cool band at equator. - Warmer bands toward 30°, 45°, 60°. - Blazing near 80°+. Equal-area projections would have a uniform “area distortion heat map” (flat), but their angular distortion heat map would show patterns.
That 132× area factor at the Web Mercator cutoff is insane. Can you compute it more precisely?

Approximate it: At φ = 85.05112878°: cos φ ≈ cos(85.0511°) ≈ 0.087 (roughly; more precisely around 0.0872) sec φ ≈ 1 / 0.0872 ≈ 11.47 sec² φ ≈ 131.6 So yes: around 130× area inflation at the cutoff. Meaning: a patch of land near that latitude, if it existed as a small region, would look ~130 times larger in map area than an equal true-area patch at the equator.
I’m now thinking about how we teach kids geography. If you had to pick one world projection for a classroom wall map, what would you pick?

If the goal is general geographic literacy without instilling a warped sense of relative sizes, I’d pick a projection that balances familiarity and area honesty: - Winkel Tripel: common in modern atlases; looks familiar; avoids extreme distortions. Not equal-area, but much less misleading than Mercator. - Equal Earth: if you want to explicitly teach area truth and support thematic understanding, while still looking pleasant. - Mollweide/Eckert IV: if you want a clear equal-area message, accepting a more “projected” look. If you pick one, I’d lean Equal Earth for a “truthful about size” classroom map, or Winkel Tripel if you want maximum familiarity and broad acceptance while reducing the worst biases.
Can we do a “projection debate” between Equal Earth and Winkel Tripel? Like, what do you gain/lose?

Equal Earth: - Gain: true area everywhere. Great for any “how much” question. - Lose: angles/shapes; local shape distortion exists, though designed to be mild. - Visual: slightly more “equal-area vibe,” but still familiar. Winkel Tripel: - Gain: very balanced look; often minimal overall distortion by a combined heuristic; shapes look quite natural. - Lose: area is not exact; size comparisons are still somewhat biased (though far less than Mercator). - Visual: extremely familiar in atlases; good for reference. If your map will be used for interpreting thematic data (population, climate, land cover), Equal Earth is safer. If it’s a general reference map where people will point at places and recognize outlines, Winkel Tripel is a strong choice.
I want to go further: can a projection be “optimized” for a particular dataset, like minimizing distortion where most people live?

Yes—this is a real idea: design projections that minimize distortion over a weighted region. Approaches: 1) Choose standard parallels/lines to cover the region of interest. - For conic projections, pick standard parallels spanning the populated latitudes of interest. 2) Use “minimum error” projections - Some projections are designed by minimizing an objective function over the globe or a region. - You can weight the objective by population density: errors in densely populated areas count more. 3) Use adaptive or multi-scale projections - For interactive maps, you can change projection with scale or region (though this can be cognitively jarring). - Or use local projections per region (like UTM zones) for analysis. The catch: - A projection optimized for “where people live” will likely distort oceans and sparsely populated regions more. - That might be acceptable for some thematic purposes, but it’s a value choice.
That’s fascinating. Is there a projection that literally uses population weights?

Not a standard “named” projection widely adopted as a default, but the concept appears in: - Cartograms: distort area to represent population (or GDP, etc.). That’s beyond projection; it’s data-driven warping. - Custom minimum-distortion projections: academic and specialized cartography sometimes uses weighted optimization, but they’re not as mainstream as Mercator/Winkel/Mollweide. If you want “map area corresponds to population,” that’s a population cartogram. It’s mind-blowing in a different way: countries balloon/shrink based on population, not physical size. It’s intentionally not a geographic projection in the strict sense because it doesn’t preserve the Earth’s geometry constraints; it’s a thematic deformation.
Cartograms are definitely mind-blowing, but I want to stay in projection land. Let’s talk about ellipsoids. How much does the Earth-not-being-a-sphere matter for projections?

For many visual/educational maps, spherical formulas are fine. But for precise surveying, navigation, and GIS analysis, the ellipsoid matters. Earth is better approximated by an oblate spheroid: slightly flattened at the poles. WGS84 has: - semi-major axis a ≈ 6378137 m - flattening f ≈ 1/298.257223563 - eccentricity e ≈ √(2f − f²) Effects: - Distances per degree of latitude vary slightly with latitude on an ellipsoid. - Conformal projections on an ellipsoid require different formulas (e.g., ellipsoidal Mercator uses eccentricity in the y formula). - Area computations are more accurate on the ellipsoid. In Web mapping, the spherical approximation is used for speed and standardization; errors are usually tolerable for display, but not for high-precision measurements.
What’s the ellipsoidal Mercator y formula? I want to see the eccentricity show up.

For ellipsoidal Mercator (on an ellipsoid with semi-major axis a and eccentricity e), one common form is: x = a (λ − λ₀) y = a ln( tan(π/4 + φ/2) * [ (1 − e sin φ) / (1 + e sin φ) ]^(e/2) ) That extra factor involving e and sin φ corrects for the ellipsoid’s varying curvature, making the projection conformal on the ellipsoid. When e → 0 (sphere), the bracketed term → 1, and you recover the spherical Mercator formula.
This is exactly the kind of technical candy I wanted. Now, I keep hearing “conformal means scale is same in all directions.” Is that literally true?

Locally, yes: conformal implies isotropic scaling at a point—same scale factor in every direction—though the scale factor can vary from point to point. In terms of the differential: - The Jacobian at a point acts like a rotation + uniform scaling (a similarity transformation), not a shear or anisotropic stretch. - That’s why circles remain circles (Tissot circles stay circles), just with different radii. So “same in all directions” is true locally. Globally, the scale factor changes with location, so distances between far points are not preserved.
Does Mercator have the same scale in all directions at a given latitude? Or at a given point?

At a given point (λ,φ), yes—Mercator is conformal, so the local scale is isotropic at that point. And because Mercator’s distortion depends only on latitude, that scale factor is the same for all points along the same latitude (on the spherical Mercator; on the ellipsoidal version it’s still primarily latitude-dependent). So: - At latitude φ, any small segment—no matter its direction—gets scaled by sec φ (spherical case). - That’s the “same in all directions” property.
I’m curious: is there a projection that preserves distances along all meridians and parallels? Like, can we at least get that?

Not globally. You can preserve distances along one set of lines or along specific lines, but not both families everywhere. Examples: - Equirectangular preserves distances along meridians if you use y proportional to latitude (in radians) and set scale appropriately—but it doesn’t preserve distances along parallels except at the equator (or another standard parallel in the equidistant cylindrical variant). - Equidistant cylindrical projection can preserve distances along meridians and along a chosen standard parallel, but not all parallels. So you can get: - True distances from a point (azimuthal equidistant), - True distances along certain lines (equidistant projections), but not a global “grid of true distances” in both directions.
So many trade-offs. I want to play with an extreme: what’s the “least distorted” world map in some sense?

“Least distorted” depends on the chosen metric, but for general world maps, a few projections are often cited as having low overall distortion: - Winkel Tripel: widely regarded as one of the best compromise projections for whole-world maps; used by many atlases. - Natural Earth: another compromise designed for pleasing aesthetics and balanced distortion (not to be confused with the Equal Earth projection). - Kavrayskiy VII: a compromise with good overall balance; less common in popular atlases but liked in cartography circles. But if you define a specific objective function (e.g., minimize RMS angular distortion + RMS area distortion), you can compute an “optimal” projection under that criterion. The result might not be a standard named projection, and it might look unfamiliar. So practically, Winkel Tripel is often the answer for “best general-purpose world map,” while Equal Earth is the answer for “best general-purpose equal-area world map.”
Tell me about Natural Earth projection. How is it different from Robinson or Winkel Tripel?

Natural Earth is a compromise pseudo-cylindrical projection designed for small-scale thematic mapping, especially to show the whole world with a pleasing shape and moderate distortion. Differences: - Compared to Robinson: Natural Earth aims for smoother graticules and a more “natural” look of continents, with careful control of polar regions and the overall outline. Robinson is based on tabulated values originally derived empirically; Natural Earth is defined analytically (polynomial formulas). - Compared to Winkel Tripel: Winkel Tripel is constructed as an average of the Aitoff projection and equirectangular, aiming to minimize three distortions. Natural Earth is designed with aesthetic and thematic mapping needs in mind, not a specific “tripel” criterion. If Robinson is “classic compromise atlas look,” Natural Earth is “modern compromise with analytic smoothness,” and Winkel Tripel is “atlas standard with a specific combined-distortion philosophy.”
I want to understand Aitoff and Hammer projections because they show up in these hybrids. What are they?

Aitoff and Hammer are related global projections often used for world maps, especially in astronomy and some thematic contexts. Aitoff: - It’s essentially an azimuthal projection (based on equidistant) applied in a way that maps the whole sphere into an ellipse. - Not equal-area. Hammer (Hammer–Aitoff): - The Hammer projection is equal-area and maps the world into an ellipse with a 2:1 aspect ratio. - Often people call the equal-area one “Hammer” and sometimes loosely refer to both as “Hammer-Aitoff.” Why they matter: - They produce an elliptical world map with curved meridians. - Winkel Tripel uses Aitoff as one component: it averages Aitoff with equirectangular to balance distortions. So Aitoff/Hammer are part of the “family tree” of compromise and equal-area global projections.
If Hammer is equal-area, why isn’t it the default for thematic maps?

It can be, and it’s used. But defaults are driven by aesthetics, familiarity, and what distortions people tolerate. Reasons Hammer isn’t universal: - The elliptical frame is less convenient for some layouts than a rectangle. - Distortion patterns: while area is correct, shapes near the outer edges can look stretched, and some people find the overall look less familiar than newer designs like Equal Earth. - Tradition and inertia: Mollweide and Eckert have long histories in thematic cartography. That said, Hammer is a solid equal-area projection, especially when you want an elliptical world and you care about area fidelity.
Let’s do a distortion comparison between Mollweide and Hammer. Are they similar?

Both are equal-area and elliptical, but they distribute shape distortion differently. Mollweide: - Parallels are straight lines (horizontal), evenly spaced in a particular way. - Meridians are ellipses meeting at the poles. - Distortion is moderate near the center; edges can stretch. Hammer: - Meridians and parallels are more uniformly curved. - Often perceived as having a slightly different “balance” of shape distortion, with some regions looking less squashed than in Mollweide, depending on latitude and longitude. If you look at Tissot indicatrices: - Both keep equal area (ellipse areas constant). - The ellipticity pattern differs: Mollweide tends to have certain latitudinal bands where shapes look better; Hammer can distribute distortion more smoothly in some senses. Choosing between them is often aesthetic and task-driven.
I’m thinking about longitude discontinuities. Why is ±180° always the “cut”? Could we cut somewhere else?

±180° is conventional because it’s opposite the Greenwich meridian (0°) and aligns with the International Date Line conceptually. But mathematically, you can cut anywhere by choosing a different central meridian. For any global projection that maps longitudes into a finite x-range, you must choose a place where longitude wraps around. The “cut” is where the mapping jumps from +180° to −180° (or equivalent). You can cut elsewhere to: - Keep a region contiguous (e.g., center a map on Europe to avoid splitting Eurasia). - Put the cut in the ocean to avoid splitting continents. - Emphasize a different ocean basin. So yes: the cut is a design choice, not a necessity tied to 180° specifically.
I want to do a “what if”: What if we insisted on preserving area and angles at the equator only, and gradually blending? Is there a projection that blends properties?

Many compromise projections can be seen as blending behaviors: they don’t preserve a single property globally but are tuned to have small errors in multiple properties. But “preserve area and angles at the equator only” is possible in the sense that at a single line (or point), you can match multiple properties because constraints are local. - Along the equator, you can set scale so that area distortion is 1 and angular distortion is small (or even zero at points). - Away from the equator, you can choose functions that balance the growth of different distortions. Winkel Tripel is literally a blend (average) of two projections. Other projections use weighted averages or polynomial fits to minimize error. However, you cannot have a projection that is both conformal and equal-area in a neighborhood (not just on a line), unless it’s an isometry up to scale that would imply impossible curvature matching. But matching along a line is fine.
So you can “cheat” along a line, but not in a region. That makes sense. What about local projections used in GIS—why do people use different projections for different countries?

Because distortion grows with the size and placement of the region relative to the projection’s “sweet spot.” Local/regional projection selection: - Choose a projection whose low-distortion region covers your area of interest. - Align the projection’s geometry with the region’s orientation. Examples: - A country that’s wider east–west in mid-latitudes: Albers Equal-Area Conic or Lambert Conformal Conic with two standard parallels bracketing the country. - A country that’s tall north–south: Transverse Mercator, with central meridian through the country. - A region around a city: a local conformal projection or a local tangent plane approximation. GIS also uses standardized coordinate reference systems (CRS) so datasets align. That’s why you see national grids: they’re optimized for that country’s extent and mapping needs.
Can you explain UTM in projection terms? I know it’s Transverse Mercator, but what makes it special?

UTM (Universal Transverse Mercator) is a global system that divides Earth into 60 zones, each 6° of longitude wide. Each zone uses a Transverse Mercator projection with a central meridian down the middle of the zone. What makes it special: - It localizes distortion: within a 6° band, Transverse Mercator distortion stays small. - It uses a secant cylinder: scale factor at the central meridian is slightly less than 1 (k0 = 0.9996), so there are two lines within the zone where scale is exactly 1. This reduces maximum distortion across the zone. - It provides a consistent metric grid (easting/northing in meters) useful for engineering and mapping. So UTM is a practical compromise: rather than one projection for the whole world, it uses many local projections stitched together by zone boundaries.
If UTM is so practical, why don’t we just use a bunch of zones for everything?

For analysis and engineering, people often do exactly that—use local projections per region. But for a single global map or global dataset, zones create problems: - Discontinuities at zone boundaries: coordinates jump; features crossing zones become awkward. - Global visualization: you want a single continuous coordinate space to render and tile. - Data integration: global layers are easier in one CRS. So zone systems are great for local accuracy, but awkward for global continuity and web mapping.
Let’s go back to the “mind-blown country sizes” thing. Give me a list of surprising area comparisons that Mercator messes up.

Here are several that tend to surprise people, with true area facts (approximate): 1) Africa vs Greenland - Africa ~30.37 million km² - Greenland ~2.16 million km² Africa ~14× larger. 2) South America vs Europe - South America ~17.84 million km² - Europe ~10.18 million km² South America ~1.75× larger. 3) Democratic Republic of the Congo vs Western Europe countries - DRC ~2.34 million km² - It’s larger than the combined area of several prominent Western European countries, yet often looks smaller on Mercator maps than you’d “feel.” 4) India vs Scandinavia - India ~3.29 million km² - Scandinavia (as a region) can look comparable or larger on Mercator depending on framing, but India is enormous. 5) Indonesia vs Alaska - Indonesia ~1.90 million km² - Alaska ~1.72 million km² Indonesia is larger, but Alaska can look bigger on Mercator. 6) Australia vs Greenland - Australia ~7.69 million km² - Greenland ~2.16 million km² Australia ~3.6× larger, yet Greenland can visually compete on Mercator. 7) China vs USA (contiguous) - China ~9.6 million km² - USA ~9.8 million km² (total), contiguous ~8.1 million km² They’re comparable, but depending on projection, one can feel larger. Mercator’s main “mess-up” is systematically inflating high latitudes, so comparisons involving Canada, Russia, Scandinavia, and Greenland against tropical regions are especially misleading.
It’s interesting that people rarely get surprised by Australia’s size—it’s low latitude-ish but still looks big. Is that because it’s isolated and easy to see?

Exactly. Australia’s size is cognitively reinforced because: - It’s a single, isolated landmass with clear outline. - It’s often shown without being crowded by neighbors. - It sits in mid-to-low latitudes, so Mercator doesn’t distort it as violently as high-latitude regions. - Cultural familiarity: many people hear “Australia is continent-sized,” so the map matches prior expectations. Contrast that with, say, the Congo Basin or Indonesia: - They’re in crowded equatorial regions with many nearby features. - They’re often visually “compressed” under the dominance of inflated higher latitudes in common map layouts. So the surprise is less about absolute depiction and more about relative prominence.
I want to understand how “map scale bars” work on distorted projections. On Mercator, can you even have a meaningful scale bar?

You can, but it’s only meaningful in a limited sense. On any projection with variable scale: - There is no single map scale that applies everywhere. - A scale bar is only accurate at certain locations (or along certain lines). On Mercator: - Scale is true at the equator (classic tangent form). - At latitude φ, the scale factor is sec φ, so distances on the map correspond to sec φ times the equatorial scale. So a scale bar on a Mercator map is: - Accurate at the equator (or at the chosen standard parallel if you use a secant version). - Increasingly wrong away from it. Some maps address this by: - Not using a scale bar at all for world Mercator maps. - Using a “scale varies with latitude” note. - Providing multiple scale bars for different latitudes. For local Mercator maps (small latitude range), the variation can be small enough that a single scale bar is acceptable.
That makes me think: when I zoom into a web map, does the projection distortion change?

The projection itself doesn’t change in Web Mercator (it stays the same), but your perception changes because: - At large scales (zoomed in), you’re seeing a smaller region where distortion variation across the view is smaller. - At small scales (zoomed out), you see the full latitude range, and distortion differences are dramatic. Also, web maps often use: - Different data generalization and rendering at different zoom levels. - Sometimes different local coordinate calculations for labels and styling. But fundamentally, the same projection is used across zooms; it’s just that distortion is less noticeable when you’re looking at a small patch.
I’m obsessed with the idea that Mercator “stretches north-south” too, not just east-west. People often say it just makes Greenland wide, but it’s also tall, right?

Right—Mercator scales uniformly in all directions at a point (conformal), so it stretches both east–west and north–south equally by sec φ. That means: - A square region stays a square (shape preserved), but the square’s size increases. - So high-latitude regions aren’t just wider; they’re larger overall, with both dimensions inflated. The “wide Greenland” impression often comes from comparing it to equatorial Africa on a rectangular map, but the distortion is not directional bias; it’s isotropic scaling at each point. Plate Carrée is the one that makes things “wider but not taller” with latitude.
Okay, now I want to talk about “projection literacy.” What should people know to not be fooled?

A compact projection literacy toolkit: 1) Ask: what property is preserved? - Conformal: angles/shapes locally. - Equal-area: area. - Equidistant: distances from a point/along lines. - Azimuthal: directions from a point. - Compromise: none exactly. 2) Look at latitude placement - High-latitude exaggeration is common in cylindrical projections like Mercator. 3) Use a few anchor facts - Africa is huge (~30M km²). - South America > Europe. - Greenland is much smaller than it appears on Mercator. 4) Recognize the graticule - Rectangular grid with poles missing: likely Mercator/Web Mercator. - Oval world: likely equal-area or compromise pseudo-cylindrical. - Radial lines and circles: azimuthal. 5) For data maps, demand equal-area - Especially choropleths where shading implies quantities per unit area. 6) Remember centrality bias - What’s centered feels central; edges feel marginal. That’s enough to avoid the biggest cognitive traps.
Now I want a super visual explanation without images: describe what happens to a circle drawn around the North Pole on different projections.

Imagine a small circle on the globe centered at the North Pole—a “cap” boundary at some latitude, say 80°N (a parallel). Mercator: - Parallels are horizontal straight lines. - The 80°N parallel becomes a straight horizontal line across the map. - But because Mercator can’t show the pole, the “cap” between 80°N and 90°N becomes an infinite strip in y as you approach 90°; in practice it’s truncated. So a polar circle isn’t a circle at all—it’s a line, and the polar region becomes massively stretched vertically. Polar stereographic (centered on the pole): - Parallels become circles around the center. - The 80°N parallel is a nice circle around the pole. - Shapes near the pole are good (conformal), but areas grow as you move outward. Lambert azimuthal equal-area (centered on the pole): - The 80°N parallel is also a circle. - The area of the polar cap is correct. - Shapes distort as you move outward, but the “cap” feels like a coherent region. Mollweide: - The 80°N parallel is a straight line segment (since parallels are straight). - The polar region is compressed into the top part of the oval; it doesn’t explode. So: in azimuthal polar projections, polar circles look like circles (intuitively satisfying). In Mercator, they become lines and the pole becomes unreachable.
That helps. Another visual: what happens to a straight line on the map—what does it correspond to on the globe? Like on Mercator, a straight line is a rhumb line, but what about on Mollweide?

On Mollweide, a straight line on the map does not correspond to a single simple class of curves on the globe the way it does for Mercator or gnomonic. It’s a more complex relationship. General statement: - In most projections, straight lines on the map correspond to complicated curves on the globe. - Only certain projections have the special property that some important class of spherical curves map to straight lines: - Gnomonic: great circles. - Mercator: rhumb lines. - Some azimuthal projections: great circles through the center can map to straight lines (e.g., azimuthal equidistant: geodesics from center are straight radials). Mollweide is designed for equal area, not for “straight line meaning.” So a straight segment you draw is typically not a geodesic or rhumb line; it’s just a straight segment in that coordinate system. That’s why route maps rarely use Mollweide for navigation semantics.

Makes sense. I’m curious about conformal vs equal-area in terms of differential geometry. Is there a clean statement like “conformal preserves the metric up to scale” and “equal-area preserves the area form”?

Yes, that’s a clean way to say it. Conformal: - The projection transforms the surface metric g into a scalar multiple of the Euclidean metric locally: g_map = k(λ,φ)² * g_surface (in appropriate local coordinates) - Meaning: angles are preserved; lengths are scaled uniformly by k at that point. Equal-area: - The projection preserves the area element (2-form): dA_map = dA_surface * constant_scale - Meaning: the determinant of the Jacobian, when compared properly to the surface area element, is constant (usually 1 in normalized units). You can’t satisfy both globally because that would imply preserving both metric angles and area, which would require preserving curvature in a way the plane cannot match.
I want to talk about “projection families.” Are there infinite projections? How do cartographers even categorize them?

There are infinitely many projections—uncountably many, really—because you can define countless smooth functions mapping (λ,φ) to (x,y). Cartographers categorize them by construction and properties: By developable surface: - Cylindrical - Conic - Azimuthal (planar) - Pseudo-cylindrical - Pseudo-conic - Polyhedral (like Dymaxion) - Other / miscellaneous By preserved property: - Conformal - Equal-area - Equidistant - Azimuthal (direction-preserving from a point) - Compromise By aspect/orientation: - Normal (equator horizontal) - Transverse (rotated 90°) - Oblique (arbitrary tilt) By interruption: - Interrupted vs uninterrupted By form: - Analytical (closed-form equations) - Tabular (like Robinson originally) - Numerical/optimized So classification is like biology: families, genera, hybrids.
Dymaxion! That’s the Buckminster Fuller one, right? How does that fit in?

Yes—Dymaxion is a polyhedral map, usually based on an icosahedron. Concept: - Project the globe onto the faces of an icosahedron (20 triangles). - Unfold the icosahedron into a plane in a chosen net arrangement. Properties: - Distortion is relatively low on each face because each face covers a small region. - Distortion concentrates along edges where faces meet (discontinuities). - You can choose an unfolding that keeps most land connected and minimizes ocean splits, creating a strong “one island Earth” impression. It’s not conformal or equal-area globally in the classic sense; it’s a different strategy: distribute distortion and accept seams. Dymaxion maps are mind-blowing because they break the rectangular world expectation and show how “the frame” itself is a design choice.
What would Tissot indicatrices look like on a polyhedral projection?

They’d be piecewise-defined and discontinuous across face boundaries. Within a face: - Indicatrices would vary smoothly depending on the projection used onto that face (often some form of gnomonic or other mapping onto the polyhedron face). At edges: - There’s a sudden change because adjacent faces are unfolded with a dihedral angle that becomes a sharp fold in the plane. - So indicatrices would “jump” in orientation and possibly size when you cross an edge. So instead of a smooth field of ellipses across the whole world, you’d see a patchwork: relatively tame ellipses inside faces, abrupt changes at boundaries.
I feel like the more we talk, the more I realize “a map is an argument.” Even the choice of seam is an argument.

Exactly. Even without explicit intent, a map makes choices that imply priorities: - What stays connected vs what gets cut. - What sits at the center. - What looks large or small. - What looks familiar or alien. - What’s emphasized by the frame shape (rectangle, oval, polyhedron net). Those choices affect perception. That’s why cartography is both mathematics and rhetoric: the transformation is mathematical, but the presentation is communicative.
I want a final big mind-blown synthesis: if you could narrate a “tour of distortions” as if the map is stretching a rubber Earth, how would you describe Mercator vs equal-area vs compromise?

Imagine the Earth painted on a perfectly elastic rubber skin. Mercator: - You pin the rubber along the equator to a cylinder and start rolling it out flat. - To keep tiny shapes from shearing (to keep angles right), you have to stretch the rubber more and more as you move toward the poles. - By the time you reach high latitudes, the rubber is under extreme tension—it’s been inflated in all directions. - The poles can’t be laid down without infinite stretching, so they rip off to infinity. Equal-area (like Mollweide/Equal Earth): - You impose a rule: the rubber’s area can’t change. No part is allowed to inflate or shrink in total area. - Since you still need to flatten it, you allow the rubber to shear: circles become ellipses, angles change. - The “amount of rubber” representing each region stays honest, but the shapes slide and squish to make it fit. Compromise (like Winkel Tripel/Robinson): - You accept that you’ll violate all the strict rules a little. - You stretch some, shear some, compress some, but you try to avoid any region being grotesquely mangled. - The result is psychologically smooth: nothing screams “this is broken,” but nothing is perfectly truthful either. That’s the tour: Mercator keeps the paint strokes’ angles pristine by stretching the rubber; equal-area keeps the rubber’s area fixed by letting the paint strokes skew; compromise tries to keep both the rubber and the paint from looking too tortured.
That’s the vibe I wanted. Before we end, give me one practical tip for spotting Mercator in the wild without reading the projection label.

Look for these telltales: - Straight, evenly spaced horizontal latitude lines, with spacing that grows rapidly toward the top/bottom. - Greenland and high-latitude landmasses looking disproportionately huge. - The map cuts off around ±85° latitude (often you won’t see the poles). - Meridians are straight vertical lines, equally spaced. - The whole world is a rectangle. If you see a rectangular world with a uniform grid and missing poles, it’s almost certainly Mercator/Web Mercator.
What will the next supercontinent look like? Is Pangaea Ultima the leading theory?

The short version is: there isn’t a single “leading” theory in the sense of a consensus forecast, but there are a few end-member scenarios that geodynamic modelers keep revisiting, and “Pangaea Ultima” (also called “Pangaea Proxima” in some older popularizations) is one of them.
